title: GraphWalker: Agentic Knowledge Graph Question Answering via Synthetic Trajectory Curriculum
abstract: Agentic knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) requires an agent to iteratively interact with knowledge graphs (KGs), posing challenges in both training data scarcity and reasoning generalization. Specifically, existing approaches often restrict agent exploration: prompting-based methods lack autonomous navigation training, while current training pipelines usually confine reasoning to predefined trajectories. To this end, this paper proposes \textit{GraphWalker}, a novel agentic KGQA framework that addresses these challenges through \textit{Automated Trajectory Synthesis} and \textit{Stage-wise Fine-tuning}. GraphWalker adopts a two-stage SFT training paradigm: First, the agent is trained on structurally diverse trajectories synthesized from constrained random-walk paths, establishing a broad exploration prior over the KG; Second, the agent is further fine-tuned on a small set of expert trajectories to develop reflection and error recovery capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our stage-wise SFT paradigm unlocks a higher performance ceiling for a lightweight reinforcement learning (RL) stage, enabling GraphWalker to achieve state-of-the-art performance on CWQ and WebQSP. Additional results on GrailQA and our constructed GraphWalkerBench confirm that GraphWalker enhances generalization to out-of-distribution reasoning paths. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/XuShuwenn/GraphWalker

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) aims to answer natural language questions by reasoning over large-scale structured Knowledge Graphs (KGs) (Jang et al. , 2017 ) , where world knowledge is encoded as (subject, predicate, object) triples. Answering complex questions over KGs demands multi-hop reasoning, systematic path planning, and structural awareness of the underlying graph topology. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) (Brown et al. , 2020 ) has opened new opportunities for KGQA due to their strong language understanding and reasoning capabilities (Petroni et al. , 2019 ; Xiong et al. , 2026b ) .

As illustrated in Figure 1 , traditional KGQA methods follow two main paradigms: Information Retrieval (IR) methods (Zhang et al. , 2022 ; Dong et al. , 2023 ; Li et al. , 2024 ) , which retrieve relevant subgraphs and extract answers from the gathered evidence, and Semantic Parsing (SP) methods (Berant et al. , 2013 ; Yih et al. , 2015 ; Li et al. , 2023 ; Luo et al. , 2024a ) , which translate questions into executable logical forms (e.g., SPARQL) to query KGs directly.
However, both paradigms confine reasoning within a static, pre-defined scope, failing to accommodate the scale, noise, and long-tail entity distributions of real-world KGs (Auer et al. , 2007 ; Bollacker et al. , 2008 ; Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014 ; Xiong et al. , 2025 ) .

To overcome this limitation, Agentic KGQA methods empower LLMs to iteratively interact with the global KG via successive tool calls, dynamically refining their reasoning based on real-time KG feedback (Yao et al. , 2023 ; Sun et al. , 2024 ) .
Despite this progress, as shown in Table 1 , existing approaches remain limited in several key aspects. Prompting-based methods (Sun et al. , 2024 ; Xu et al. , 2024 ) lack the dedicated parameter updates needed to robustly navigate noisy KGs. Training-based methods , on the other hand, fail to unify key properties: some (e.g., RoG) restrict reasoning to predefined workflows, lacking autonomous interaction ; others (e.g., KG-R1) rely on pre-sampled subgraphs rather than the global KG ; and pure SFT agents (e.g., KG-Agent) suffer from superficial pattern imitation, lacking the RL optimization needed to break through the demonstration ceiling.
Besides, recent studies have consistently shown that RL optimization requires a competent SFT prior: without sufficient exploration capacity, the agent cannot discover rewarding trajectories, rendering policy optimization ineffective (Guo et al. , 2025 ; Zhang et al. , 2026 ; Xiong et al. , 2026a ) .
This raises a central question: How can an agent build a robust exploration foundation on KGs to push the boundaries of its reasoning capacity?

To address this issue, we propose GraphWalker , a novel agentic KGQA framework that autonomously synthesizes trajectories and trains the agent via Stage-wise Fine-tuning . We introduce a two-stage SFT pipeline followed by lightweight RL with a sparse exact-match (EM) reward.
First, we conduct Constrained Random Walk (CRW) on the KG and synthesize GraphSynth , a corpus of 15k diverse trajectories to establish a broad exploration prior.
Second, we construct GraphRoll , a dataset of 6k distilled expert trajectories that equips the agent with reflection and error recovery.
Importantly, we demonstrate that the first stage serves as a mid-training exploration scaffold , which unlocks a higher performance ceiling after RL.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

• We identify training data scarcity as a core bottleneck in agentic KGQA generalization, and propose GraphWalker to address this limitation through two complementary datasets: GraphSynth , a corpus of 15k autonomously constructed, structurally diverse agentic trajectories, and GraphRoll , a curated collection of 6k high-quality expert trajectories. • We introduce a two-stage SFT pipeline: the first stage on GraphSynth establishes a broad exploration prior over the KG, while the second stage on GraphRoll equips the agent with reflection and error recovery capabilities, effectively raising the performance ceiling for both RL optimization and test-time inference. • Extensive experiments on WebQSP and CWQ demonstrate that GraphWalker achieves SOTA performance. Strong zero-shot results on GrailQA (zero-shot) and our constructed GraphWalkerBench further confirm that our pipeline substantially broadens the agent’s search space and improves generalization to unseen structures.

sections.heading: 2GraphWalker Framework
sections.text: In this section, we present the GraphWalker framework, which consists of three components: the agentic environment, the data construction pipeline, and the training procedure. Figure 2 provides an overview of the full data construction and training pipeline.

sections.heading: 2.1Agentic Initialization
sections.text:
Environment. Following prior agentic KGQA systems (Sun et al. , 2024 ; Jiang et al. , 2025 ) ,
we formalize the KG as an executable environment backed by a SPARQL endpoint. Unlike approaches that operate on pre-defined subgraphs, the agent interacts directly with the global KG in real time during both training and inference.
This design exposes the agent to the full structural complexity of real-world KGs, including large branching factors and long-tail relations, which encourages more robust reasoning strategies.

Toolbox. To interact with this environment, we equip the agent with two primitive tools:
(1) get_relations ​ ( e ) \textbf{get\_relations}(e) returns all relations incident
to entity e e , allowing the agent to survey the local neighborhood
before selecting a retrieval direction.
(2) get_triples ​ ( e , ℛ ′ ) \textbf{get\_triples}(e,\mathcal{R}^{\prime}) returns all triples
whose head or tail is e e and whose relation belongs to a subset ℛ ′ ⊆ ℛ { e } \mathcal{R}^{\prime}\subseteq\mathcal{R}_{\{e\}} selected from the
preceding get_relations observation, enabling targeted
and efficient KG traversal.

Agent Loop. At each turn t ≤ T t\leq T , where T T is the maximum number of
interaction turns, we condition the agent on the current state s t = ( q , τ t − 1 ) s_{t}=(q,\tau_{t-1}) , where q q is the input question and τ t − 1 = { ( c i , a i , o i ) } i = 1 t − 1 \tau_{t-1}=\{(c_{i},a_{i},o_{i})\}_{i=1}^{t-1} is the interaction
history up to the preceding turn. Here, c i c_{i} denotes the model’s
internal reasoning process at turn i i , a i a_{i} the executed action,
and o i o_{i} the resulting observation.
Given s t s_{t} , the agent produces a response consisting of two parts:
(1) an internal reasoning process c t c_{t} , enclosed in <think> … </think> , which reflects on the current
state and determines the next action; and (2) an action a t a_{t} , which
is either a KG retrieval query enclosed in <kg-query> … </kg-query> , or a final answer
enclosed in <answer> … </answer> when sufficient
evidence has been gathered.
The executor processes a t a_{t} on the KG 𝒢 \mathcal{G} and returns the
retrieval result as an observation o t o_{t} , enclosed in <information> … </information> and appended to
the trajectory. The full turn record ( c t , a t , o t ) (c_{t},a_{t},o_{t}) is then
appended to τ t \tau_{t} .

sections.heading: 2.2Data Construction
sections.text:
In this section, we describe the construction of two complementary training corpora, GraphSynth-15k and GraphRoll-6k .
GraphSynth-15k focuses on broad structural coverage for exploration, while GraphRoll-6k emphasizes reflection and error recovery.

sections.heading: 2.3GraphWalker Training
sections.text:
We adopt a two-stage SFT cold start followed by RL training.
The first stage equips the agent with general exploration skills,
the second cultivates reflection and error recovery, and RL
subsequently aligns long-horizon decision making.
We parameterize the agent policy as an autoregressive language
model π θ \pi_{\theta} .
Specifically, in stage 1 and stage 2, the model is optimized using a next-token prediction objective over agentic interaction sequences, while treating <information> blocks as environmental context rather than learning targets: ℒ ​ ( θ ) = − ∑ t ∈ ℐ agent log ⁡ P θ ​ ( x t ∣ x < t ) , \mathcal{L}(\theta)=-\sum_{t\in\mathcal{I}_{\text{agent}}}\log P_{\theta}(x_{t}\mid x_{<t}), (2) where ℐ agent \mathcal{I}_{\text{agent}} indexes tokens in the <think> , <kg-query> , and <answer> blocks.

SFT Stage 1: Learning to Reason and Explore on Diverse KGs.

In stage 1, we first train the agent on GraphSynth-15k . This stage aims to internalize robust navigation behaviors and
reliable tool-invocation patterns under realistic retrieval noise,
while treating KG retrieval results as environmental observations.

SFT Stage 2: Learning to Reflect and Recover from Errors.

In stage 2, we fine-tune the model on GraphRoll-6k .
Unlike stage 1, which emphasizes broad exploration, the expert trajectories in GraphRoll-6k contain explicit instances of error detection and reasoning recovery, guiding the model to develop more deliberate and self-corrective interaction strategies.

RL Stage: Unlocking the Performance Ceiling

Building on the two-stage SFT cold-start model, we apply
RL to push the agent’s performance beyond the SFT ceiling
through autonomous exploration.
We train the model on a uniformly sampled CWQ subset using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) (Guo et al. , 2025 ) with a sparse, trajectory-level exact match reward: R ​ ( τ ) = s EM ​ ( τ ) ∈ { 0 , 1 } . R(\tau)=s_{\text{EM}}(\tau)\in\{0,1\}. (3) Since our two-stage SFT already establishes an exploration and reflection prior, this sparse reward is sufficient to drive further policy improvement without dense intermediate supervision. The GRPO objective function is:

𝒥 GRPO ​ ( θ ) = 𝔼 q , { τ ( i ) } ​ [ 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N 1 | τ ( i ) | ​ ∑ j = 1 | τ ( i ) | min ⁡ ( ρ i , j ​ A ^ i , j , clip ​ ( ρ i , j , 1 − ϵ , 1 + ϵ ) ​ A ^ i , j ) ] − β ​ 𝔻 KL ​ ( π θ ∥ π ref ) , \mathcal{J}_{\text{GRPO}}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{q,\{\tau^{(i)}\}}\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{|\tau^{(i)}|}\sum_{j=1}^{|\tau^{(i)}|}\min\!\left(\rho_{i,j}\hat{A}_{i,j},\;\mathrm{clip}(\rho_{i,j},1-\epsilon,1+\epsilon)\,\hat{A}_{i,j}\right)\right]-\beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi_{\theta}\|\pi_{\text{ref}}), (4)

where ρ i , j = π θ ​ ( a i , j | s i , j ) π old ​ ( a i , j | s i , j ) \rho_{i,j}=\frac{\pi_{\theta}(a_{i,j}|s_{i,j})}{\pi_{\text{old}}(a_{i,j}|s_{i,j})} is the importance ratio, A ^ i , j \hat{A}_{i,j} is the group-normalized
advantage, and π ref \pi_{\text{ref}} is the two-stage SFT model used as the KL reference policy.

sections.heading: 3Experiments
sections.text:
In this section, we conduct comprehensive experiments and report the experimental setup, main results, and ablation analyses.
We answer the following research questions (RQs): RQ1: Does GraphWalker outperform other LLM-based KGQA methods? RQ2: Does GraphSynth broaden GraphWalker’s search space and improve generalization? RQ3: How does each training stage contribute to GraphWalker’s final performance? RQ4: How do the main components of our data construction pipeline work?

sections.heading: 3.1Experimental Setup
sections.text:
Datasets and Metrics. Following previous research (Sun et al. , 2024 ; Luo et al. , 2024b ) , we evaluate GraphWalker on two widely used KGQA benchmarks: CWQ (Talmor and Berant, 2018 ) and WebQSP (Yih et al. , 2016 ) . To ensure a fair comparison, we utilize the gold topic entities provided by the benchmarks and report standard Exact Match (EM) and F ​ 1 F1 scores (Talmor and Berant, 2018 ) . Details of the datasets are described in Appendix C .

Models and Baselines. We compare GraphWalker with seven previous prompting-based and training-based KGQA methods.
To better evaluate the effectiveness of various methods on knowledge reasoning tasks, we use the full Freebase as a KG backend ( Global KG setting).
This setting is more challenging than approaches based on predefined subgraphs, as the agent must navigate the full KG.
For KG-R1, we evaluate the released checkpoint in the official repository under our Global KG setting.
We additionally evaluate strong proprietary LLMs, including DeepSeek-V3.2 and GPT-4o-mini , under the GraphWalker agent framework.
Details for all baselines are provided in Appendix D .

Implementation Details. We train and evaluate GraphWalker on three open-source backbone models: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (Qwen et al. , 2024 ) , and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (Dubey and others, 2024 ) .
For SFT data collection, we use GPT-4o-mini to generate trajectories for both GraphSynth and GraphRoll. For evaluation, we set the max turn number T = 10 T=10 .
Our training implementation details are shown in Appendix G .

sections.heading: 3.2Main Results (RQ1)
sections.text:
As shown in Table 2 , GraphWalker achieves SOTA performance across all benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness from two perspectives:

Superiority in Complex Reasoning. GraphWalker consistently outperforms both prompting and training baselines.
Notably, it surpasses the GPT-4-powered GoG by 4.4% EM on CWQ, and outperforms training-based models such as KG-Agent (+7.4% EM on CWQ) and KBQA-o1 (+15.7% EM on WebQSP).
Under the more challenging Global KG setting, GraphWalker-3B-SFT-RL outperforms KG-R1 (same Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct backbone) by +4.1% EM on CWQ and +1.4% EM on WebQSP, suggesting that KG-R1’s reliance on pre-extracted subgraphs undermines its robustness.
Moreover, equipping proprietary LLMs ( GPT-4o-mini and DeepSeek-V3.2 ) with our interaction framework yields substantial gains over their IO Prompt counterparts (+17.9% and +19.7% EM on CWQ).
Impressively, GraphWalker with trained backbones outperforms these proprietary LLMs across all model sizes, demonstrating the effectiveness of our synthetic agentic curriculum and training strategy.

Cross-task Transferability. Despite being trained exclusively on CWQ, GraphWalker effectively zero-shot transfers to WebQSP (91.5% EM), outperforming all baselines trained directly on WebQSP data. This confirms that training on CWQ’s structurally diverse, high-complexity paths equips the agent with highly transferable exploration strategies.

sections.heading: 3.3Comparison Analysis (RQ2)
sections.text:
Pass@ k k Analysis. To validate that GraphSynth effectively broadens the agent’s reasoning search space, we sample 500 questions from the CWQ test set and evaluate exploration capacity via Pass@ k k over 32 32 runs.
As shown in Figure 4 , within both the SFT-only and SFT-RL settings, removing GraphSynth ( w/o SFT Stage 1 ) consistently reduces Pass@ k k across all k k , confirming its critical role in expanding the agent’s exploration capacity. In contrast, models trained with GraphSynth exhibit higher Pass@ k k values as k k increases, indicating that our CRW synthesis establishes a broader exploration prior.
Furthermore, GraphWalker-SFT-RL achieves the highest Pass@ k k across all k k , suggesting that a stronger exploration foundation enables RL to further amplify these gains.

Zero-shot Generalization. To assess whether GraphSynth effectively improves reasoning generalization, we evaluate GraphWalker and its ablated variants on two zero-shot benchmarks: the zero-shot split of GrailQA and GraphWalkerBench , a structurally diverse evaluation set constructed to include unseen reasoning structures not present in GraphSynth (see Appendix C ).
As shown in Table 4 , both SFT stages are crucial for out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Removing GraphSynth ( w/o SFT Stage 1 ) significantly impairs generalization (6.80% average EM drop), confirming its essential role in broadening the agent’s search space.
Similarly, removing GraphRoll ( w/o SFT Stage 2 ) leads to substantial performance degradation (11.10% average EM drop), as the model’s ability to self-correct is substantially weakened. Furthermore, the consistent improvement of GraphWalker-Full over w/o RL confirms that RL further enhances the generalization capability established by the SFT stages.

sections.heading: 3.4Analysis on Each Training Stage (RQ3)
sections.text:
Ablation of Each Stage. To understand the contribution of each training stage, we ablate
individual components from the training pipeline.
As shown in Table 4 , removing GraphSynth ( w/o SFT Stage 1 ) causes a substantial decline (5.10% average EM drop), indicating its critical role in establishing broad exploration priors for navigating complex KG structures.
Meanwhile, removing GraphRoll ( w/o SFT Stage 2 ) leads to an even greater drop (9.00% average EM drop), demonstrating that
GraphRoll’s expert trajectories are essential for instilling reflection and self-correction capabilities.
Finally, removing RL results in a severe 10.40% average
EM drop, confirming that RL further consolidates the capabilities
established by the SFT stages.

Importance of Stage-wise Training. To validate the proposed curriculum, we compare stage-wise training against a Mixed baseline combining GraphSynth and GraphRoll into a single SFT pass with identical RL training. As shown in Table 4 , mixed training consistently underperforms (dropping from 79.6% to 70.2% EM on CWQ) despite using identical data, confirming that training order is critical:
Stage 1 must first establish broad exploration priors before Stage 2
can effectively instill reflection and recovery capabilities.

sections.heading: 3.5Ablation Study (RQ4)
sections.text:
To better understand the internal mechanisms of GraphWalker, we
conduct ablation experiments on key components of our data pipeline.

Impact of Quality Filtering. As shown in Figure 5 , removing the quality filter from the GraphWalker-SFT model causes substantial performance drops on both datasets: EM drops from 68.3% to 46.2% on CWQ and from 82.0% to 55.6% on WebQSP, confirming that filtering out low-quality questions is critical to the overall data pipeline.

Impact of Data Scale.

We investigate the effect of data scale on each training stage separately.
For Stage 1, we vary the size of GraphSynth across { 5 ​ k , 10 ​ k , 15 ​ k } \{5\text{k},10\text{k},15\text{k}\} samples; for Stage 2, we vary GraphRoll across { 0.5 ​ k , 1 ​ k , 2 ​ k , 3 ​ k , 6 ​ k } \{0.5\text{k},1\text{k},2\text{k},3\text{k},6\text{k}\} samples.
Here, Retrieval Rate measures the proportion of test cases where the gold answer appears in the agent’s retrieved observations, reflecting the agent’s ability to find relevant evidence through KG exploration; Recovery Rate measures the proportion of cases where the
agent successfully corrects an erroneous <kg-query> step
and recovers to the correct answer.
As shown in Figure 6 , both EM and the corresponding Retrieval/Recovery Rates improve consistently with data scale, confirming the benefit of scaling trajectory supervision.
Notably, the performance gap between the full model and the ablated variants ( w/o GraphRoll and w/o GraphSynth ) persists across all scales, demonstrating that the two stages provide complementary and irreplaceable contributions.

sections.heading: 4Related work
sections.text:
Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA). To address the limitations of unstructured text retrieval and LLM hallucination, KGs have been widely adopted in question answering tasks for their ability to provide structured world knowledge (Baek et al. , 2023 ; Xu et al. , 2023 ) .
Traditional KGQA methods fall into two categories: Information retrieval (IR) methods (Zhang et al. , 2022 ; Dong et al. , 2023 ; Li et al. , 2024 ) retrieve subgraphs from KGs and extract answers from the gathered evidence, while Semantic parsing (SP) methods (Berant et al. , 2013 ; Yih et al. , 2015 ; Li et al. , 2023 ; Luo et al. , 2024a ) translate questions into logical forms (e.g., SPARQL) for verifiable execution.
However, both are brittle under noisy or incomplete KG schemas and struggle to handle long-tail entities, motivating the shift toward agentic paradigms.

Agentic KGQA. Agentic KGQA treats LLMs as autonomous agents that interact with
KGs through iterative exploration and retrieval. Prompting-based methods such as ToG (Sun et al. , 2024 ) and GoG (Xu et al. , 2024 ) perform iterative graph traversal but rely on predefined workflows or costly closed-source LLMs. Fine-tuning methods learn from curated training trajectories: CoT-based approaches (Wu et al. , 2024 ; Zhao et al. , 2024 ) enhance step-by-step reasoning transparency, RoG (Luo et al. , 2024b ) grounds plans in KGs, and KG-Agent (Jiang et al. , 2025 ) employs multi-agent reasoning but
remains limited to synthesized program data. Reinforcement learning methods such as KG-R1 (Song et al. , 2025 ) optimize the retrieval policy end-to-end but still rely on subgraph pre-extraction.

sections.heading: 5Conclusion
sections.text:
In this paper, we propose GraphWalker, a novel agentic KGQA framework that addresses training data scarcity and generalization bottlenecks via automated trajectory synthesis and stage-wise fine-tuning.
Through our two-stage SFT curriculum, the agent first establishes a broad exploration prior over diverse KG structures, and subsequently acquires critical reflection and error recovery capabilities. Notably, this robust foundation unlocks a higher performance ceiling for subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) optimization.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that GraphWalker achieves
state-of-the-art performance and strong zero-shot generalization
to unseen reasoning topologies, confirming its effectiveness and
scalability for autonomous reasoning over global KGs.

sections.heading: B.2Semantic Contamination Analysis
sections.text:
To preclude knowledge leakage in GraphSynth-15k, we compute the maximum cosine similarity between each synthetic question and all test questions across WebQSP, CWQ, and GrailQA using BGE-M3 (Chen et al. , 2024 ) .
At a strict threshold of 0.85, only 2.57% of GraphSynth questions exhibit high semantic overlap with any test question, and all flagged samples are excluded from the final training corpus.
The elevated overlap rate against CWQ at threshold 0.80 (23.30%) reflects expected domain proximity rather than leakage, as CWQ encompasses the most diverse relations and broadest domain coverage among the three benchmarks.
Table 5 and Figure 7(a) present the full results.

sections.heading: B.3Statistics of GraphSynth
sections.text:
In this section, we characterize GraphSynth-15k along two dimensions to validate its suitability as a training corpus.
Structurally, as summarized in Table 6 , GraphSynth spans five reasoning structure types, covering multi-hop compositions (2- to 5-hop) and conjunctions (2I), with 3-hop and 2I patterns accounting for over 70% of samples while longer chains ensure exposure to complex reasoning patterns.
Semantically, the synthesized questions are grounded in Freebase relations drawn from diverse topical domains, achieving broad coverage of real-world entity types and relation schemas.
As visualized in Figure 8 , the question embeddings of GraphSynth spread across a wide region of the semantic space, overlapping substantially with both CWQ and WebQSP without concentrating around any single benchmark cluster, confirming that the corpus is neither narrowly specialized nor artificially constrained to a particular domain.
Together, these properties ensure that GraphSynth provides structurally and semantically diverse supervision for Stage 1 training.

sections.heading: E.1Ablation of LLM-based Relation Reranking
sections.text:
The relation reranking module serves a critical role in our framework:
since BM25 retrieval can return a large number of candidate relations,
feeding them directly into the LLM would result in excessively long contexts
and distract the agent with irrelevant information.
To investigate its effectiveness and determine the optimal candidate threshold k k , we conduct an ablation study on CWQ and WebQSP using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as
the filter model, with results summarized in Table 8 .

Notably, even without reranking ( w/o Reranking ), GraphWalker remains
competitive, achieving 74.8% EM on CWQ and 87.0% EM on WebQSP. This
demonstrates the robustness of our interaction framework even under degraded
retrieval conditions, where the agent relies solely on BM25-retrieved relations
without any LLM-based filtering.

Nevertheless, incorporating reranking consistently improves performance, and
the choice of k k reveals a clear trend. Increasing k k from 10 to 20 yields
steady gains, as a larger candidate pool improves the recall of crucial
relations that may be missed by lower- k k settings. The Top-20 setting
achieves the best overall balance, attaining the highest EM on both CWQ
(79.6%) and WebQSP (91.5%). However, further expanding to Top-30 reverses this trend: although retaining more candidates reduces false negatives,
it simultaneously introduces excessive noise into the context, distracting the
agent during trajectory exploration and ultimately degrading performance.
Consequently, we adopt Top-20 as the default threshold for our framework.

sections.heading: E.2Case Study
sections.text: In this section, we present a representative case comparing three model variants: GraphWalker-SFT-GraphSynth , GraphWalker-SFT-GraphRoll , and GraphWalker-SFT-RL . We analyze their reasoning trajectories on one question from CWQ: “Where is the home stadium of the team who won the 1946 World Series championship?” As shown in Appendix E.3 , GraphWalker-SFT-GraphSynth successfully retrieves the correct triple [St. Louis Cardinals, arena_stadium, Busch Stadium] at Turn 3, and even further verifies it via an additional query at Turn 4, explicitly confirming “Busch Stadium is the home venue for the St. Louis Cardinals” in its reasoning, yet it outputs [‘‘St. Louis Cardinals’’] instead of extracting the stadium name—this reveals that Stage 1 SFT (on GraphSynth) teaches KG navigation but not answer extraction. GraphWalker-SFT-GraphRoll identifies the champion St. Louis Cardinals at Turn 2 and observes Sportsman’s Park in the event locations, but directly outputs this historical venue [‘‘Sportsman’s Park’’] without further exploring the team’s current stadium relation—this shows that Stage 2 SFT (on GraphRoll) alone, trained on short expert trajectories, lacks the multi-hop exploration capacity needed for deeper reasoning paths. GraphWalker-SFT-RL correctly identifies the champion at Turn 2, then directly queries the St. Louis Cardinals’ stadium relation and outputs [‘‘Busch Stadium’’] as the final answer (EM=1.0), demonstrating that RL consolidates the exploration capacity of Stage 1 and the reflection capability of Stage 2 into a more efficient and accurate reasoning strategy.

sections.heading: E.3Complete Reasoning Trajectories
sections.text: We present the complete reasoning trajectories of all four models. Each trajectory is displayed in a table where different message types are color-coded: thinking , KG queries , information feedback , errors , and final answers.

sections.heading: F.1Entity Resolution
sections.text: Entity names are resolved to MIDs via exact match on type.object.name@en . Multiple candidates are disambiguated by selecting the entity with most types. A case-insensitive fallback is applied if needed. Results are cached. Listing 1 details this process.

sections.heading: F.2get_relations
sections.text: get_relations(entity) returns relation IDs. We split outgoing/incoming queries to avoid timeout, merge results, apply whitelist filtering, and optionally rank by BM25 similarity to return top- k . CVT flatten relations are appended. See Listing 2 .

sections.heading: F.3get_triplesand CVT Flattening
sections.text:
get_triples(entity, relations) returns triples. For each relation, we query tails/heads using GROUP BY and SAMPLE to obtain one name per node. CVT nodes (no name or ID-only name) are flattened: we collect their relations, form two-hop names (e.g., rel1.rel2 ), and resolve via two-hop queries. Flatten relations are cached for future get_relations calls.
Details are shown in Listings 3 – 4 .

figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of three KGQA paradigms.

Figure 2: Overview of our proposed GraphWalker. (a) Data Construction: a multi-phase pipeline for GraphSynth-15k and outcome-based rejection sampling for GraphRoll-6k. (b) Two-Stage SFT: Stage 1 establishes a broad exploration prior, while Stage 2 instills reflection and error recovery. (c) RL Optimization: GRPO with a simple exact-match reward unlocks a higher performance ceiling enabled by the two-stage SFT prior.
Figure 3: Representative interaction paths generated by constrained random walks.
Table 3: Zero-shot performance on Grail- QA and GraphWalkerBench , which involves unseen structures in GraphSynth .
Table 4: Ablation study on CWQ and WebQSP .
Figure 5: Ablation study on the quality filter in GraphSynth Phase 2.
Figure 6: Impact of data scale on performance. Left : scaling GraphSynth data improves EM and Retrieval Rate . Right : scaling GraphRoll data improves EM and Recovery Rate .
(b) Semantic cosine similarity distribution on each benchmark.
Figure 8: t-SNE visualization of GraphSynth, CWQ, and WebQSP question embeddings, illustrating the broad semantic coverage of the synthesized corpus.

Figure 9: Fine-grained EM performance on GraphWalkerBench across question
types and ablation variants. Left : grouped bar chart. Right : heatmap. IP and PI are unseen conjunction structures
not present in GraphSynth training data.
